title: Subarrays With Exactly K Distinct
description:
Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the number of subarrays that contain exactly k distinct integers. Solve in linear time using the at most trick.

    

Constraints:
    - 1 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10^5
- 1 ≤ k ≤ len(nums)
explanation:
Intuition
Counting exactly k distinct directly is difficult. It is easier to count the number of subarrays with at most k distinct and subtract the number with at most k-1 distinct. Each at most function can be computed in O(n) using a frequency map and a sliding window that contracts when the distinct count exceeds the limit.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Define a helper to count subarrays with at most K distinct.
2. Main Algorithm: Scan with a frequency map. When distinct exceeds K, move left and decrement until valid. Add the window size to the result at each step.
3. Return Result: Compute atMost(k) - atMost(k-1) to get the exact count.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the window grows and shrinks in a
  single pass without revisiting elements more than a constant number of
  times.
- Space Complexity: O(n) to maintain counts, sets, or running
  aggregates needed to validate the window.

Key Insights
- Exactly equals can be expressed as the difference of two at most counts
- Each element is added and removed from the window a constant number of times

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to add window length to the accumulator
- Not resetting counts when they reach zero

testCases.explanation: There are 7 subarrays with exactly 2 distinct
All subarrays since only one value

hints: Compute at most K distinct with a window and counts
Remember to add the current window length at each step
